title: Are artificial sweeteners making you fat?

chunks.title: Introduction to Artificial Sweeteners
chunks.summary: The podcast begins with an overview of the speaker's views on artificial sweeteners and their potential harms. He contrasts them with non-processed sugars like those found in fruits and honey, arguing that the latter can be beneficial.
chunks.transcript: there is no place in the human diet for artificial sweeteners and corollary to that there are no Harms eating non-processed forms of sugars in this week's podcast I wanted to talk in multiple papers that I will show you in this podcast and at the beginning of the podcast I will make a case that you should not fear sugar in things like fruit and honey and maple syrup or even chunks.title: Concerns about Artificial Sweeteners
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses specific concerns regarding popular artificial sweeteners such as sucralose and aspartame. He presents evidence suggesting these sweeteners can disrupt gut microbiomes and impair glucose tolerance.
chunks.transcript: two you might be diabetic how could I possibly suggest that things like fruit or honey or maple syrup could be good for diabetics shouldn't they be using artificial sweeteners and my answer is still a resounding no they should not be using artificial sweeteners the amount of carbohydrates in their diet could be lower than yours or mine depending on your activity level but I still think it's fine for diabetics to get fruit honey maple syrup in their diets because artificial sweeteners are going to worsen diabetics insulin sensitivity in the long run I'm going to show you all that data in this podcast so let's go ahead and jump in I want to start with diabetics because I think this is really the sharp end of the spear and this is where people are going to get most hung up so consider this guys when you are diabetic a diagnosis is made with a fasting blood glucose a fasting blood glucose of 126 or more diagnoses you as diabetic there's no real criteria for what your fasting insulin should be to be diabetic and I think this is where we've gone wrong but nevertheless fasting blood glucose is a reasonable metric it's a reasonable proxy for your insulin sensitivity

chunks.title: Natural Sugars vs. Artificial Sweeteners
chunks.summary: A case is made for the incorporation of natural sugars like honey, fruit, and maple syrup into diets, even for diabetics. The speaker emphasizes that these sugars do not have the same negative effects as artificial sweeteners.
chunks.transcript: diabetes is a disease of insulin resistance that is a really important point to understand that first and foremost diabetes is a disease of insulin resistance and because you are insulin resistant your blood sugar goes up I think that all too often in nutritional circles people conflate blood sugar with diabetes or conflate blood sugar with a cause of diabetes because blood sugar goes up as a result of diabetes but your blood glucose is not coming from the foods you eat in diabetes the blood glucose is mostly coming from inappropriate gluconeogenesis at the level of the liver because you are insulin resistant remember that so the Crux the center of your diabetes is not that you're eating too much sugar I promise you that it is that you are insulin resistant and your liver is making too much blood glucose for your body so with that in mind and I will show you some really interesting articles here in a moment I would say diabetics don't need to completely avoid

chunks.title: Case Studies on Honey and Diabetes
chunks.summary: The speaker reviews a study on the effects of honey consumption in diabetic patients. Results show improved insulin sensitivity and weight loss in those incorporating honey into their diet.

chunks.title: Conclusion and Dietary Recommendations
chunks.summary: In closing, the speaker reiterates his stance that artificial sweeteners should be eliminated from diets. He encourages listeners to embrace whole food sources of carbohydrates, like fruits and honey, for better health outcomes.
